title: Managing ADHD with Apple and Siri
text:
Growing up in Silicon Valley, Apple was everywhere. After owning various Apple products for almost 14 years and experimenting with the ecosystem and its capabilities, I have learned a variety of strategies that help me manage my ADHD. Before diving into these, I would like to preface this post with the observation that having multiple Apple products is part of why I find Apple tools so helpful. iCloud makes syncing with my phone, watch, iPad, and laptop incredibly easy. When I set a reminder for a Calendar event, I will receive a notification not only on my phone, but also on my watch, iPad, and laptop. It’s okay if I don’t hear my phone remind me of an appointment because I can hear a notification from my watch and feel the notification on my wrist. If I don’t hear or feel my watch, then perhaps I’m hyperfocused on something on my iPad or laptop. Multiple devices receiving the same notification means there are backups if the alert from one device goes overlooked.
Three essential tools of the Apple ecosystem that I have used to great benefit are widgets, Siri, and Apple HomeKit.

title: Managing ADHD with Apple and Siri
text:
Apple Watch Complications
When I got my first Apple Watch, my primary watch face was Activity Digital. It was January 2021, and COVID-19 was rather conducive to a sedentary lifestyle. I like data and numbers, so the information tracked on my Apple Watch would help motivate me to get up and move. However, Activity Digital was soon superseded in favor of another watch face, Modular .
While attending UCSD, my calendar noted lectures, discussions, office hours, and even exams. Event alerts are useful, but there were simply too many events on my calendar. Modular allowed my to see my schedule at a glance, and tapping the complication opened my Calendar if I needed further information.

title: Managing ADHD with Apple and Siri
Managing ADHD with Apple and Siri

Managing ADHD with Apple and Siri
text:
Lights
I use smart (dimmer) switches or smart plugs so that I can ask Siri to turn on or turn off my lights. I set automations for turning my lights off in case I accidentally fall asleep before turning them off myself. Currently, the automations are set for every hour, on the hour. Since I also have difficulty noticing or feeling time passing, I find that the lights turning off every hour (even if I turn them back on again) helps me mark the passing of time.

title: Managing ADHD with Apple and Siri
text:
Other
I get very cold very easily, so I love using a space heater to make my room nice and toasty. Generally, I am especially mindful with heat-generating appliances and I double check that I have turned things off when I am done using them. Whereas I can only check again that I have turned off the stove when I am done cooking, the space heater is an easy opportunity for a smart-plug safeguard. I can set the plug to turn off when I leave home. With the space heater plugged into the smart plug, which is plugged into the outlet, the heater will turn off when I leave home even if I forgot to turn it off manually. title: Research
text:
I am currently writing my MA thesis on identity, affect, and split ticket voting in the 2016, 2020, and 2024 elections (see Figure 1). I build on Lilliana Mason’s (2015) concept of partisan-ideological sorting as well as Davis and Mason’s (2016) research. My research is based on the expectation that different patterns in levels of sorting, strength of affect toward the parties, and strength toward the parties’ respective candidates should lead to different probabilities of voting a split ticket.



Figure 1







During the Specialization phase of my research master program (fall 2025), I explored affective polarization, civic duty, and voter turnout (see Figure 2). Previous research has looked at affective polarization and voter turnout, and civic duty and voter turnout. My research used the 2020 ANES Time Series Study to look at both affective polarization and civic duty as two predictors of turnout.
I found that the influence of civic duty on turnout varies at different degrees of affective polarization. Figure 3 shows that affective polarization and a (strong) sense of civic duty were associated with greater probabilities of voting, but a strong sense of duty alone was also associated with a greater probability of voting, even when affective polarization was absent or minimal.



Figure 2









Figure 3: Turnout Probabilities by Degree of Affective Polarization and Level of Civic Duty










References

Davis, N. T., & Mason, L. (2016). Sorting and the split-ticket: Evidence from presidential and subpresidential elections. Political Behavior, 38(2), 337–354. https://doi.org/10.1007/s11109-015-9315-7


Mason, L. (2015). "I disrespectfully agree": The differential effects of partisan sorting on social and issue polarization. American Journal of Political Science, 59(1), 128–145. https://doi.org/10.1111/ajps.12089

title: Jillian Reynolds
text:
LinkedIn

Bio
I am currently a second-year research master student in the Modern History and International Relations program at the University of Groningen in the Netherlands. A research master is a two-year, research-focused program for students interested in pursuing a PhD or career in research.

After graduating high school and completing the IB Diploma Programme, I attended UC San Diego in La Jolla, California. At UCSD, I majored in Political Science/International Relations and earned minors in History and Global Health.
After earning my BA in 2023, I spent a year working as a paraeducator for a local public school district while completing the four-course R for Data Analytics certificate program through UC San Diego Division of Extended Studies.





Education
University of Groningen | Groningen, Netherlands
MA in Modern History and International Relations | Sep. 2024 - present
University of California, San Diego | San Diego, CA
BA in Political Science/International Relations | Sep. 2019 - June 2023

Minors in History and Global Health
Provost Honors: Spring 2020, Fall 2021, Winter 2021, Spring 2021, Winter 2022, Spring 2022, Fall 2022, Winter 2023, Spring 2023



Experiences
Paraeducator, San Mateo-Foster City School District; San Mateo, CA — Sep. 2023 - June 2024
Assisted the lead teacher with the district’s after school program. Supervised and led activities. Also helped with homework during homework time. Helped tidy and organize toys, books, and supplies around the classroom.
Part-Time Stock Associate, Under Armour; San Diego, CA — Aug. 2022 - Dec. 2022
Processed shipments, completed replenishment, and fulfilled online and in-store pickup orders. Assisted on sales floor.
Swim Instructor, Noonan Family Swim School; Del Mar, CA — Aug. 2021 - Nov. 2021
Taught children 2 years and older. Devised lessons to progress students’ skills (based on learn-to-swim curriculum) and assessed whether students were ready to progress to the next skill level.
Runner, UC San Diego Health; San Diego, CA — Jan. 2021 - May 2021
Volunteered at the Petco Park Vaccination Superstation. Coordinated between charge nurse and vaccinators, stocked supply carts for vaccinators, prepared vaccination kits (alcohol wipe, bandaid, vaccination card, sticker), and passed out vaccination cards.
High School Student Poll Worker, San Mateo County Assessor-County Clerk- Recorder & Elections, San Mateo County, CA — Nov. 2018
Participated in a program for eligible high school students to work Election Day at a poll. Greeted voters, facilitated paperwork, and passed out stickers.
Adoptee Volunteer, Chunhui China Care Home, Beijing, China — July 2018
The summer before my senior year of high school, I went on a trip with two other girls from my adoption group. We volunteered with other Chinese American adoptees at the Chunhui China Care Home in Beijing. The children at the home came from around the country and were awaiting or recovering from medical treatment in Beijing. The trip was organized by OneSky and China’s Children International.
